mappings.rationaleAI: Relativism from Protagoras through cultural and epistemic relativism addresses problems in epistemology (B.3.c) and criteria of meaning and truth in philosophical thought (A.4).
Truth theories from correspondence to deflationary views and Tarski's semantic theory directly address criteria of meaning and truth (A.4) and problems in epistemology (B.3.c).
The soul from Plato through Descartes to neuroscience covers problems in metaphysics (B.1.c) and views concerning the mind and its relation to the body (B.4).
The concept of the individual in Western thought from Renaissance through modernity addresses the philosophy of man and philosophical anthropology (B.5).
Consciousness, the hard problem, qualia, and the binding problem directly address the philosophy of mind (B.4) and its problems (B.4.c).
The philosophy of love from Plato's Symposium through existentialist perspectives engages with ethics (B.6) and aesthetics (B.8).
The physics of reality and quantum mechanics' philosophical implications address the philosophy of nature (B.2) and the philosophy of physics (B.2.d).
The nature of mathematics and debates about realism, formalism, and foundations address the philosophy of mathematics (B.11).
Happiness from Aristotle's eudaimonia through utilitarian hedonism addresses problems in ethics (B.6.c).
The concept of evil from theodicy through Arendt's banality of evil addresses problems in metaphysics (B.1.c) and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Freedom from Hobbes through Berlin's negative and positive liberty addresses political philosophy (B.7).
Hume's empiricism, problem of induction, and is-ought distinction address epistemology (B.3) and ethics (B.6).
The 18th-century concept of taste from Addison through Kant directly addresses aesthetics as a discipline (B.8) and approaches to aesthetic experience (B.8.b).
Duty from Stoic kathekon through Kant's categorical imperative addresses the nature of ethics (B.6) and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Beauty from Plato through Kant's disinterested judgment and modern challenges addresses aesthetics (B.8), its development (B.8.b), and problems in aesthetics (B.8.c).
The history and theory of metaphor in language and cognition directly addresses the philosophy of language (B.9).
Wittgenstein's philosophy of language from the Tractatus through the Investigations addresses the philosophy of language (B.9) and philosophy of mind (B.4).
AI and the Chinese Room argument address problems in the philosophy of mind (B.4.c) including whether machines can think.
Arendt's political philosophy including the distinction between labor, work, and action, and her concept of the public realm address political philosophy (B.7).
Russell's work in mathematical logic, theory of descriptions, and logical atomism address the philosophy of logic (B.10) and the philosophy of mathematics (B.11).
The philosophical status of laws of nature addresses the philosophy of nature (B.2) and its basic aspects of the natural order (B.2.c).
Philippa Foot's revival of virtue ethics and the trolley problem address problems in ethics (B.6.c) and the nature of ethics (B.6.a).
Plato's Gorgias examining rhetoric, justice, and power addresses ethics (B.6) and political philosophy (B.7).
Evolutionary psychology's claims about human nature address the philosophy of man and philosophical anthropology (B.5).
The nature-nurture debate addresses philosophical anthropology (B.5) and its problems (B.5.c).
Pragmatism's rejection of absolute truth in favor of practical consequences addresses diverse conceptions of philosophy (A.1) and criteria of meaning and truth (A.4).
Socrates' acceptance of death in the Phaedo and Crito represents a foundational narrative for ethical reasoning and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Plato's Republic covers metaphysics through the Theory of Forms (B.1), epistemology through the allegory of the cave (B.3), and political philosophy through the philosopher-king (B.7).
